鯵.meaning: Horse mackerel; a common food fish known as aji in Japanese.

鰯.name: Sardine
鰯.meaning: Sardine; a small fish commonly called iwashi in Japanese.
鰯.reviewNote: Checked iwashi, katakana usage, and pilchard or sardine gloss against the Japanese entry.

鰻.name: Eel
鰻.meaning: Eel; a familiar food fish known as unagi in Japanese.

鰹.name: Bonito
鰹.meaning: Bonito or skipjack tuna; a fish known as katsuo in Japanese.

魚.name: Fish
魚.meaning: Fish; a general kanji for fish.

鮭.name: Salmon
鮭.meaning: Salmon; a fish commonly called sake or shake in Japanese.
鮭.reviewNote: Checked sake, shake, katakana usage, and salmon gloss against the Japanese entry.

鯖.name: Mackerel
鯖.meaning: Mackerel; a fish known as saba in Japanese.
鯖.reviewNote: Checked saba, katakana usage, and mackerel gloss against the Japanese entry.

鯛.name: Sea bream
鯛.meaning: Sea bream; a fish known as tai in Japanese.
鯛.reviewNote: Checked tai, katakana usage, and sea bream gloss against the Japanese entry.

鱈.name: Cod
鱈.meaning: Cod; a fish known as tara in Japanese.
鱈.reviewNote: Checked tara, katakana usage, and codfish gloss against the Japanese entry.

鮪.name: Tuna
鮪.meaning: Tuna; a fish known as maguro in Japanese.
鮪.reviewNote: Checked maguro, katakana usage, and tuna gloss against the Japanese entry.

鰺.name: Horse mackerel
鰺.meaning: Horse mackerel; another kanji form used for aji.
鰺.reviewNote: Checked aji reading, kyujitai relation to 鯵, and horse mackerel gloss against the Japanese entry.

鰆.name: Japanese Spanish mackerel
鰆.meaning: A spring-associated fish commonly eaten in Japan.
鰆.note: Often written in katakana as サワラ in biological contexts.
鰆.reviewNote: Checked sawara, katakana usage, and Japanese Spanish mackerel gloss against the Japanese entry.

鮎.name: Sweetfish
鮎.meaning: Sweetfish; a river fish known as ayu in Japanese.
鮎.note: In Japanese usage, 鮎 commonly refers to ayu or sweetfish.
鮎.reviewNote: Checked ayu, katakana usage, sweetfish gloss, and Japanese-specific usage note against the Japanese entry.

鯉.name: Carp
鯉.meaning: Carp; a freshwater fish known as koi in Japanese.
鯉.reviewNote: Checked koi, katakana usage, and common carp gloss against the Japanese entry.

鮫.name: Shark
鮫.meaning: Shark; a fish known as same in Japanese.
鮫.reviewNote: Checked same, katakana usage, and shark gloss against the Japanese entry.

鯨.name: Whale
鯨.meaning: Whale; a large sea mammal known as kujira in Japanese.
鯨.note: Although written with the fish radical, whales are mammals.
鯨.reviewNote: Checked kujira, katakana usage, whale gloss, and organism usage note against the Japanese entry.

鰭.name: Fin
鰭.meaning: Fin; a fish body part known as hire in Japanese.
鰭.reviewNote: Checked hire reading and fin gloss against the Japanese entry.

鱒.name: Trout
鱒.meaning: Trout; a fish known as masu in Japanese.
鱒.reviewNote: Checked masu, katakana usage, and trout or sea trout gloss against the Japanese entry.

鮒.name: Crucian carp
鮒.meaning: Crucian carp; a freshwater fish known as funa in Japanese.
鮒.reviewNote: Checked funa, katakana usage, and crucian carp gloss against the Japanese entry.

鱗.name: Scale
鱗.meaning: Scale; the scales of fish, reptiles, and other animals.
鱗.reviewNote: Checked uroko, rin, and scale gloss against the Japanese entry.

鰐.name: Crocodile
鰐.meaning: Crocodile or alligator; a reptile known as wani in Japanese.
鰐.note: Older Japanese usage can also refer to sharks or other large dangerous fish.
鰐.reviewNote: Checked wani, crocodile or alligator gloss, and older shark or large dangerous fish usage note against the Japanese entry.

鰍.name: Loach or bullhead
鰍.meaning: A fish kanji with Japanese readings for loach, young yellowtail, and bullhead.
鰍.note: The Japanese readings point to different fish names, so the public gloss stays broad.
鰍.reviewNote: Checked dojo, inada, kajika, shu, and the loach, young yellowtail, and bullhead glosses against the Japanese entry.

鮮.name: Fresh or vivid
鮮.meaning: Fresh, vivid, clear, or brilliant; a common non-fish meaning under the fish radical.
鮮.note: Common Japanese use is adjectival, as in fresh or vivid, rather than a fish name.
鮮.reviewNote: Checked sen, azayaka, atarashii, and the fresh or vivid glosses against the Japanese entry.

魯.name: Lu or foolish
魯.meaning: Lu as a historical proper name, and foolish or dull in older usage.
魯.note: This fish-radical character is not a fish name in common Japanese use.
魯.reviewNote: Checked ro, ru, oroka, the Lu historical use, and non-fish meaning against the Japanese entry.

魴.name: Bream or John Dory
魴.meaning: A fish kanji associated with bream or John Dory; Japanese readings include kagamidai and oshikiuo.
魴.note: The Japanese entry lists readings but leaves the definition unfilled, so the English gloss follows the fish glosses on the same source page.
魴.reviewNote: Checked kagamidai, oshikiuo, ho, bo, plus the bream and John Dory fish glosses on the source page.

鮓.name: Sushi
鮓.meaning: Sushi; an older or variant kanji spelling for vinegared rice served with fish, vegetables, or other toppings.
鮓.note: Used as an alternative spelling of sushi and closely related to 鮨.
鮓.reviewNote: Checked sushi, sha, sa, alternative-spelling note, and the sushi gloss against the Japanese entry.

鮃.name: Olive flounder
鮃.meaning: Olive flounder; a flatfish known as hirame in Japanese.
鮃.note: Alternative spellings include 平目 and 比目魚.
鮃.reviewNote: Checked hirame, katakana usage, hei, hyo, alternative spellings, and olive flounder gloss against the Japanese entry.

鮑.name: Abalone
鮑.meaning: Abalone; an edible marine mollusk known as awabi in Japanese.
鮑.note: Although written with the fish radical, abalone is a mollusk.
鮑.reviewNote: Checked awabi, shiouo, ho, byo, and abalone gloss against the Japanese entry.

鮗.name: Dotted gizzard shad
鮗.meaning: Dotted gizzard shad; a fish known as konoshiro in Japanese.
鮗.note: This kokuji is mainly tied to konoshiro, with a rarer irregular hiiragi use also listed.
鮗.reviewNote: Checked kokuji status, konoshiro, the dotted gizzard shad gloss, and the irregular hiiragi listing against the Japanese entry.

鮟.name: Anglerfish or monkfish
鮟.meaning: Anglerfish or monkfish; used in compounds such as 鮟鱇 for anko.
鮟.note: Only found in compounds; the familiar fish name is 鮟鱇.
鮟.reviewNote: Checked an reading, anglerfish or monkfish gloss, 鮟鱇 compound, and only-in-compounds usage note against the Japanese entry.

鮠.name: Dace
鮠.meaning: Dace; a freshwater carp-family fish read haya or hae in Japanese.
鮠.note: Chinese and Korean senses point to catfish, but the Japanese entry gives dace.
鮠.reviewNote: Checked haya, hae, gai, Japanese dace gloss, and cross-language catfish distinction against the source page.

鮨.name: Sushi
鮨.meaning: Sushi; a kanji spelling for vinegared rice served with fish, vegetables, or other toppings.
鮨.note: Listed as an alternative spelling of the same sushi entry as 鮓.
鮨.reviewNote: Checked sushi, shi, gi, ki, the sushi gloss, and the alternative-spelling relationship with 鮓 against the Japanese entry.

鮴.name: Rockfish, sculpin, or goby
鮴.meaning: A fish kanji with Japanese readings for rockfish, sculpin or bullhead, freshwater goby, and a rare shellfish sense.
鮴.note: The gori reading is listed as probably the most common, while kochi and mate are rare uses.
鮴.reviewNote: Checked mebaru, gori, kochi, mate, the rockfish, sculpin, goby, flathead, and rare shellfish glosses, plus the gori usage note against the Japanese entry.

鯀.name: Gun or giant fish
鯀.meaning: Gun, a figure in Chinese mythology; historical dictionaries also gloss the character as a giant fish.
鯀.note: The Japanese entry lists only the on reading, so the public meaning follows the sourced Chinese definitions.
鯀.reviewNote: Checked kon reading, the historical giant fish gloss, and Gun mythology use against the source page.

鯊.name: Goby or shark
鯊.meaning: Goby in Japanese usage, with same also listed as a shark reading and shark as the Chinese gloss.
鯊.note: The Japanese noun section documents haze as goby, while Chinese usage documents shark.
鯊.reviewNote: Checked haze, same, sha, sa, Japanese goby gloss, and Chinese shark gloss against the source page.

鯆.name: Ray or dolphin
鯆.meaning: Ray or skate in Chinese; a rare Japanese spelling for dolphin or porpoise read iruka.
鯆.note: The Japanese iruka use is rare, and dolphins and porpoises are mammals despite the fish radical.
鯆.reviewNote: Checked iruka, saba, fu, ho, the rare dolphin or porpoise Japanese gloss, and the ray or skate Chinese gloss against the source page.

鯏.name: Japanese littleneck clam
鯏.meaning: Japanese littleneck clam or Manila clam; a shellfish read asari in Japanese.
鯏.note: Although written with the fish radical, the main Japanese gloss is a clam.
鯏.reviewNote: Checked asari, ugui, and the Manila clam, Japanese littleneck clam, Japanese cockle, and Japanese carpet shell glosses against the Japanese entry.

鯑.name: Herring roe
鯑.meaning: Herring roe; fish eggs known as kazunoko in Japanese.
鯑.reviewNote: Checked kazunoko reading and herring roe gloss against the Japanese entry.

鯒.name: Flathead
鯒.meaning: Flathead; a fish known as kochi in Japanese.
鯒.note: 鮴 is listed as an alternative spelling for this kochi sense.
鯒.reviewNote: Checked kochi, katakana usage, flathead gloss, and the 鮴 alternative spelling note against the Japanese entry.

鯣.name: Dried squid
鯣.meaning: Dried squid or cuttlefish; a seafood foodstuff known as surume in Japanese.
鯣.note: The dried squid sense is now most common; older usage can also refer to Japanese flying squid.
鯣.reviewNote: Checked surume, eki, yaku, dried squid or cuttlefish gloss, Japanese flying squid sense, and common-sense note against the Japanese entry.

鯢.name: Salamander
鯢.meaning: Salamander; also a kanji sense for female whale and small fish.
鯢.note: Although written with the fish radical, the main Japanese noun sense is a salamander.
鯢.reviewNote: Checked sanshouo, mekujira, ge, gei, the salamander noun, and female whale and small fish kanji senses against the Japanese entry.

鯤.name: Mythical giant fish
鯤.meaning: A giant fish from Chinese mythology, known in Japanese with the on reading kon.
鯤.note: The Japanese noun entry describes a northern-sea giant fish from Chinese mythology.
鯤.reviewNote: Checked kon reading, large mythical fish kanji sense, roe sense, and the Chinese-mythology giant fish noun against the Japanese entry.

鯔.name: Flathead mullet
鯔.meaning: Flathead mullet; a coastal fish known as bora in Japanese.
鯔.note: The fish has size- and region-dependent names: todo for large adults, ina for juveniles, and nayoshi as an archaic name.
鯔.reviewNote: Checked bora, todo, ina, nayoshi, shi, flathead mullet gloss, katakana usage, and age or regional naming notes against the Japanese entry.

鯡.name: Herring
鯡.meaning: Herring; also a kanji sense for roe or fish eggs.
鯡.note: The Japanese entry lists 鰊 as a synonym for the herring sense and 鮞 for the roe sense.
鯡.reviewNote: Checked nishin, hararago, harago, hi, herring gloss, roe or fish eggs sense, and synonym notes against the Japanese entry.

鯱.name: Orca or shachihoko
鯱.meaning: Orca or killer whale; also shachihoko, a tiger-headed carp-like roof ornament in Japanese mythology.
鯱.note: A kokuji; the shachi sense is an orca, while shachihoko is a mythical roof ornament.
鯱.reviewNote: Checked shachi, shachihoko, shatchoko, ko, kokuji status, orca or killer whale gloss, and the shachihoko mythology sense against the Japanese entry.

鯰.name: Catfish
鯰.meaning: Catfish, especially Amur catfish; also an idiomatic earthquake sense in Japanese.
鯰.note: A kokuji; the biological term is often written in katakana as ナマズ.
鯰.reviewNote: Checked namazu, katakana usage, nen, kokuji status, catfish and Amur catfish glosses, and the idiomatic earthquake sense against the Japanese entry.

鰕.name: Shrimp or prawn
鰕.meaning: Shrimp, prawn, lobster, crayfish, or another long-tailed decapod crustacean read ebi in Japanese.
鰕.note: An alternative spelling of 海老; although written with the fish radical, the main Japanese sense is a crustacean.
鰕.reviewNote: Checked ebi, ge, ka, the 海老 cross-reference, shrimp, prawn, lobster, crayfish, and long-tailed decapod crustacean glosses, plus alternative spellings against the Japanese entry.

鰔.name: Codfish
鰔.meaning: Codfish; a rare alternative spelling of 鱈 read tara.
鰔.note: The source lists 鰔 as a rare alternative spelling for the tara or codfish entry; karei is also listed as a kun reading.
鰔.reviewNote: Checked tara, karei, ken, kon, gen, kan, codfish gloss through the 鱈 cross-reference, and rare alternative spelling note against the Japanese entry.

鰉.name: Higai fish
鰉.meaning: Higai; a Japanese fish name identified with Sarcocheilichthys variegatus.
鰉.note: The kanji entry also lists sturgeon and carp senses, while the Japanese noun entry gives higai.
鰉.reviewNote: Checked higai, katakana usage, ko, the Sarcocheilichthys variegatus noun entry, usage note, and sturgeon or carp kanji senses against the Japanese entry.

鰓.name: Gill
鰓.meaning: Gill; the breathing organ of fish and other aquatic animals.
鰓.note: Alternative spellings listed in the source include 腮 and 顋.
鰓.reviewNote: Checked era, katakana usage, sai, shi, gill gloss, and alternative spellings 腮 and 顋 against the Japanese entry.

鰌.name: Pond loach
鰌.meaning: Pond loach; an alternative spelling of 泥鰌 read dojo.

鰈.name: Righteye flounder
鰈.meaning: Righteye flounder; a flatfish known as karei in Japanese.

鰈.reviewNote: Checked karei, jo, to, cho, righteye flounder gloss, flatfish family note, and the rare obsolete sole sense against the Japanese entry.

鰒.name: Abalone or blowfish
鰒.meaning: Abalone in dated usage, or blowfish; read awabi or fugu in Japanese.

鰊.name: Pacific herring

鰮.name: Sardine

鰮.reviewNote: Checked iwashi, on, kyujitai relationship to 鰛, alternative-spelling relationship to 鰯, and pilchard or Japanese sardine gloss against the Japanese entry.

鰛.name: Sardine
鰛.meaning: Sardine or Japanese pilchard; a shinjitai form read iwashi in Japanese.
鰛.note: Shinjitai form of 鰮 and an alternative spelling of 鰯.
鰛.reviewNote: Checked iwashi, on, shinjitai relationship to 鰮, alternative-spelling relationship to 鰯, and pilchard or Japanese sardine gloss against the Japanese entry.

鰥.name: Widower
鰥.meaning: Widower; a non-fish social term written with the fish radical.

鰥.reviewNote: Checked yamome, yamoo, ken, kan, kon, the widower Japanese sense, and the obsolete yellowcheek Chinese sense against the source page.

鰤.name: Japanese amberjack
鰤.meaning: Japanese amberjack or yellowtail; the adult fish known as buri in Japanese.
鰤.note: The source notes size- and region-dependent names for this fish; kamasu is listed as a separate barracuda spelling sense.
鰤.reviewNote: Checked buri, katakana usage, shi, kamasu, Japanese amberjack, yellowtail, adult-fish sense, size or regional naming note, and barracuda alternative-spelling sense against the Japanese entry.

鰰.name: Sailfin sandfish
鰰.meaning: Sailfin sandfish; a fish known as hatahata in Japanese.
鰰.note: 鱩 is listed as an alternative spelling; ina is listed as a nanori reading.
鰰.reviewNote: Checked hatahata, katakana usage, ina, sailfin sandfish gloss, 鱩 alternative spelling, and kaminariuo synonym against the Japanese entry.

鰲.name: Mythological sea turtle
鰲.meaning: Huge mythological sea turtle; read oogame or go in Japanese.
鰲.note: Although written with the fish radical, the sourced meaning is a mythological turtle, not a fish.
鰲.reviewNote: Checked oogame, go, the huge mythological sea turtle Japanese sense, and the Chinese mythology sea turtle definition against the source page.

鱆.name: Octopus
鱆.meaning: Octopus; a seafood mollusk read tako in Japanese.
鱆.note: Although written with the fish radical, octopus is a mollusk.
鱆.reviewNote: Checked tako, sho, Japanese image caption, Chinese octopus definition, and 鱆魚 compound against the source page.

鰾.name: Swim bladder
鰾.meaning: Swim bladder; a buoyancy organ in fish read ukibukuro in Japanese.
鰾.note: Alternative spellings listed in the source include 浮き袋, 浮袋, and 浮き囊.
鰾.reviewNote: Checked ukibukuro, byo, hyo, swim bladder gloss, and alternative spellings 浮き袋, 浮袋, and 浮き囊 against the Japanese entry.

鱚.name: Sillaginid or whiting
鱚.meaning: Sillaginid or whiting; a fish known as kisu in Japanese.
鱚.reviewNote: Checked kisu, katakana usage, and sillaginid or whiting gloss against the Japanese entry.

鱧.name: Daggertooth pike conger
鱧.meaning: Daggertooth pike conger; an eel-like fish known as hamo in Japanese.
鱧.note: The older hamu reading is listed as archaic or possibly obsolete.
鱧.reviewNote: Checked hamo, katakana usage, hamu, rai, rei, daggertooth pike conger gloss, eel note, and archaic or obsolete hamu sense against the Japanese entry.

鱠.name: Namasu
鱠.meaning: Namasu; a dish of raw fish or vegetables dressed with vinegar.
鱠.note: Kanjipedia also lists 膾 as an alternative spelling.
鱠.reviewNote: Checked namasu, vinegar-dressed raw fish or vegetable dish meaning, and 膾 alternative spelling against the Kanjipedia word entry.

鱶.name: Large shark
鱶.meaning: Large shark; a general shark term read fuka in Japanese.
鱶.note: The source also lists a Chinese variant relationship to 鯗, meaning dried or cured fish.
鱶.reviewNote: Checked fuka, himono, yo, sho, large shark gloss, 鱶鰭 derived term, and the Chinese variant relationship to 鯗 against the source page.

鱸.name: Japanese sea bass
鱸.meaning: Japanese sea bass; a fish known as suzuki in Japanese.
鱸.note: The source notes that organism names like this are often written in katakana in biological contexts.
鱸.reviewNote: Checked suzuki, katakana usage, ru, ro, sea bass kanji gloss, Japanese sea bass noun gloss, shinjitai form 魲, and katakana usage note against the Japanese entry.

鮖.name: Kajika sculpin
鮖.meaning: Kajika; a freshwater sculpin in the family Cottidae.
鮖.note: Kanjipedia notes that the character evokes a fish living among stones.
鮖.reviewNote: Checked kajika, Cottidae freshwater fish meaning, kokuji note, and stone-place etymology note against Kanjipedia; checked kajiko in vendored Unihan_Readings.txt.

鮹.name: Octopus
鮹.meaning: Octopus; an alternate fish-radical spelling read tako in Japanese.
鮹.note: Although written with the fish radical, octopus is a mollusk; compare the reviewed 鱆 entry.
鮹.reviewNote: Checked tako, sho, so, and the octopus or devilfish kDefinition in vendored Unihan_Readings.txt.

鯲.name: Loach
鯲.meaning: Loach; a freshwater fish read dojo in Japanese.
鯲.note: The character is kept separate from the reviewed 鰌 entry even though both are read dojo.
鯲.reviewNote: Checked dojo in vendored Unihan_Readings.txt and checked the pond loach page for Dojo loach and Japanese dojo nabe usage.

鰄.name: Kairagi sharkskin
鰄.meaning: Kairagi; rough shark or ray skin used as a material, and by extension a sharkskin-like ceramic glaze.
鰄.note: Kairagi can also be written 梅花皮.
鰄.reviewNote: Checked kairagi, i, and shark-skin material meaning against Kanjipedia; checked fish skin, shark or stingray, ceramic glaze, and 梅花皮 alternative spelling through the Jisho JMdict entry.

鰡.name: Striped mullet
鰡.meaning: Striped or flathead grey mullet; a fish read bora in Japanese.
鰡.note: Often written 鯔; 鰡 also appears in 鰡子, karasumi or dried mullet roe.
鰡.reviewNote: Checked bora and striped or flathead grey mullet gloss in the Jisho JMdict entry for 鰡 as a spelling of 鯔; checked ryu and bora-family fish meaning against Mojinavi; checked ryu in vendored Unihan_Readings.txt; retained 鰡子 karasumi note from the Jisho JMdict entry.

鱇.name: Anglerfish
鱇.meaning: Anglerfish; the ko component of anko in 鮟鱇.
鱇.note: Often encountered as the second character of 鮟鱇, read anko.
鱇.reviewNote: Checked ko and the anglerfish kDefinition in vendored Unihan_Readings.txt.
